text.title: pnpm --filter for partial monorepo work
text.summary: How to run installs, builds, and tests against a subset of the workspace using pnpm's filter syntax.

section.title: Filter patterns
section.p: `pnpm --filter` (or `-F`) lets the agent operate on one package, on a path glob, on packages affected by a git ref, or on the dependency closure of a target. Critical for keeping inner-loop time bounded in a large monorepo.

rule.statement: Use `--filter ...{path}` (with the leading dots) to operate on a package AND all of its dependents; `--filter {path}^...` for a package and its dependencies. CI uses `--filter ...[origin/main]` to test only what changed since main.
rule.rationale: Running `pnpm test` workspace-wide on every commit is unnecessary in monorepos with dozens of packages; filtered runs cut wall-clock time 5–20x while still exercising the affected slice. The leading-dots syntax is exact and standard; agents must use it instead of greps and shell glue.

example.code:
# install only what `web` needs pnpm install --filter web^...
# test web and everything that depends on it pnpm --filter ...web test
# test only packages changed since origin/main pnpm --filter '...[origin/main]' test
# add a runtime dep to a single package, picking up the catalog version pnpm add react -F web
example.note: Filters are precise; the agent never has to glob package paths by hand.

antipattern.description: Running `pnpm -r test` on every CI run when only one package changed.
antipattern.reason: Wastes CI minutes and floods the agent's feedback loop with unrelated output. Use `--filter '...[origin/main]'` so the slice is automatic and minimal.
